summary: El bloqueo del Estrecho de Hormuz dispara el petróleo a ~$110/barril, congela los recortes de tasas globales y redefine el mapa energético y financiero del planeta.
causalChain: El conflicto en el Golfo Pérsico cortó ~20% del suministro mundial de petróleo; la energía más cara empuja la inflación hacia arriba en EE.UU., Australia y Europa; la Fed, RBA y BCE quedan atrapados sin poder recortar tasas; México amortigua el golpe con remesas récord y carry trade, pero el USMCA en julio lo es todo.

table.angle: Hormuz blockade sends oil to $110+/barrel — IEA calls it worst supply shock in history
Gold falls to $4,556 as dollar climbs; 10yr US borrowing cost hits 4.59%
Fed holds at 3.5–3.75%; tariffs + oil shock trap rate cuts — Warsh makes no promises
Banxico cuts to 6.50% — lowest since 2022, likely end of easing cycle
Q1 remesas hit $14.4B — highest quarter ever recorded since 1995
USMCA review July 2026; first negotiating round set for week of May 25
Nikkei at 59,585 as Japan posts 7th straight month of growing exports
PBOC lets yuan slide 4% despite China running a $1T+ trade surplus
Oil +84% since late 2025 hits German and Italian factories hardest
STOXX 600 up 17.3% YTD — European stocks outrun the S&P 500 in 2026
RBA hikes 3rd time this year to 4.35%; consumer confidence at 50-year low
BHP + Rio Tinto agree to jointly mine 200M tonnes of iron ore in Pilbara
Bitcoin ETFs pull $2.44B in April; BlackRock's fund alone takes $335M in one day
GENIUS Act signed; EU's MiCA 70-day deadline; Japan eyes 20% flat crypto tax
Digital dollar supply crosses $319B; Circle mints $250M USDC; Western Union launches Solana stablecoin
Stevens Institute + NIST: atomic clocks show time can exist in quantum superposition
SWISS100 (UNIGE/UNIL): centenarians' blood proteins look like 30–60-year-olds, not 80s
Earlham Institute: Oxford pond microbe breaks the universal DNA stop-signal rule

drafts.text: The world's main oil shipping lane — a narrow passage called the Strait of Hormuz — is blocked. About one-fifth of all oil used on Earth passes through there. Attacks on Gulf energy sites and a US blockade of Iranian ports pushed Brent crude to $111/barrel. The IEA calls this the single biggest oil supply shock ever recorded. More expensive oil means pricier gas, heating, shipping, and groceries — everywhere, all at once.

The US government is now paying 4.59% to borrow money for 10 years — near a one-year high. Higher returns on US dollar loans made the dollar stronger (its index climbed above 99). A stronger dollar made gold — which is priced in dollars — more expensive for foreign buyers, pushing it down to ~$4,556/oz this week. Hotter inflation from surging oil costs means investors no longer expect the Fed to lower rates anytime soon.

The US central bank is holding its key rate at 3.5–3.75%. Incoming Fed chief Kevin Warsh, nominated by Trump, says he made no promises to cut rates. The problem: import taxes already pushed prices higher, and now an oil shock is adding more. Cutting rates while prices rise would make inflation worse — so the Fed is stuck. Businesses and families face high prices AND high borrowing costs at the same time, with no relief in sight.

Mexico's central bank just cut its main interest rate to 6.50%, the lowest since 2022. The vote was close — 3 in favor, 2 for holding — signaling this is likely the last cut for a while. Inflation is still at 4.45%, above the 3% target. The 300-point gap between Mexico's rates and US rates keeps attracting international investors who park money in pesos to earn a higher return, which supports the currency.

In just the first three months of 2026, Mexicans living abroad sent home $14.4 billion — the highest single quarter ever recorded going back to 1995. That tops what Mexico earns from oil or tourism in the same period. Most of this money comes from the US. Even as the peso strengthened (making each dollar worth fewer pesos), the number of transfers kept rising, directly feeding millions of households and propping up consumer spending.

The trade deal governing everything Mexico sells to the US and Canada is up for review in July 2026. The first formal negotiating round is set for the week of May 25. Key topics: which car parts must be built in North America, energy security, and critical minerals like lithium. Nearshoring factories are already running in Monterrey and Querétaro — but the rules they operate under could change entirely if negotiations go sideways.

Japan's stock market hit 59,585 — near an all-time record — as the country posted its seventh straight month of growing exports, with a $4.18 billion trade surplus in March. Japan earns more when its currency stays weak: cars and electronics cost less abroad. But investors expecting a weaker US dollar are now buying yen, which could squeeze Japanese exporters. The Bank of Japan is watching carefully — a fast yen rise cuts directly into export earnings.

China is selling more to the rest of the world than ever — its trade surplus grew by over $1 trillion this year alone. Yet the Chinese currency got weaker by 4%. Why? China's central bank is deliberately preventing the yuan from rising, because a stronger yuan makes Chinese goods cost more abroad, hurting exports. So it is managing the exchange rate while flooding global markets with cheap products — a balancing act watched closely by trading partners everywhere.

Oil prices have jumped 84% since late 2025, and Europe's biggest industrial nations — Germany and Italy — are taking the hardest hit. Both run large factories that depend heavily on energy. Factory production in the eurozone barely grew in March (0.2% vs the 0.3% expected). Germany, which already slipped into recession last year, faces a double blow: high energy costs plus weak demand from China. Europe's central bank is stuck — cutting rates risks more inflation, keeping them high slows growth.

Despite war headlines and high energy costs, European stocks beat US stocks in 2026. The STOXX 600 — a broad index of European companies — is up 17.3% this year, outpacing the S&P 500 and Nasdaq. Why? European stocks were cheap after years of lagging behind; defense budgets surged across the continent; and the euro strengthened against the dollar. Investors who avoided Europe for a decade are now paying close attention.

Australia's central bank raised borrowing costs for the third time this year, pushing its key rate to 4.35%. The reason: global energy prices spiking because of the Hormuz crisis are fueling inflation at home too. But Australian households are already stretched — consumer confidence just hit its seventh lowest reading in 50 years of data. The country's biggest bank says Australia could tip into recession. The central bank hinted it may pause here to see how the economy absorbs the latest shock.

The world's two largest mining companies — BHP and Rio Tinto — agreed to work together in Australia's Pilbara region to dig up to 200 million tonnes of iron ore from neighboring deposits. Instead of each building separate processing plants, they will share equipment and infrastructure. Iron ore is the key ingredient in steel, and China is the world's largest buyer. This deal could lower costs and extend the life of deposits too small to mine profitably on their own.

Bitcoin investment funds traded on stock exchanges pulled in $2.44 billion in April 2026 — the best month this year. BlackRock's fund alone brought in $335 million in a single day. Ethereum funds also reversed five months of outflows, pulling in $356 million in April. This is real money from pension funds, banks, and mainstream investors — not just crypto enthusiasts. Two straight months of net inflows signals institutions now treat Bitcoin as a legitimate asset worth holding.

The US just signed a law — the GENIUS Act — setting clear rules for digital dollars (tokens that hold a stable $1 value): they must be backed 1-for-1 with real money, redeemable any time, and independently verified. In Europe, any company offering crypto services without a license must shut down by July 1 — a hard 70-day deadline. Japan is moving to cut its tax on crypto profits from up to 55% down to a flat 20%. Crypto is becoming a regulated financial industry, not a wild west.

The total supply of digital dollars — tokens that hold a stable $1 value and are used to move money across crypto markets — just crossed $319 billion. Tether leads at $189.6 billion; Circle's USDC at $77.6 billion. A fresh $250 million in USDC was minted this week. Think of these as the fuel that keeps crypto markets running — when the supply grows, it means more buying power is entering the system. Western Union also launched its own regulated digital dollar on the Solana network.

Time might tick faster and slower at the exact same moment — for the same clock. Researchers at Stevens Institute of Technology, working with teams from Colorado State University and NIST, found that ultra-precise atomic clocks could theoretically exist in two time-flow states at once, obeying the same quantum laws as particles that exist in two places simultaneously. It is like Schrödinger's cat, but for time itself — and lab tests may soon be possible. 📄 Published in Physical Review Letters · More: https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260517211440.htm

People who live past 100 have blood that looks chemically younger than people in their 80s. A team from the University of Geneva and University of Lausanne measured 37 blood proteins in Swiss centenarians and found their inflammation levels, fat metabolism markers, and cellular stress signals all resemble 30–60-year-olds far more than octogenarians. The SWISS100 study is the first large centenarian research project in Switzerland — and its findings may one day help slow aging for everyone. 📄 Published in Aging Cell · More: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12883144/

At the Earlham Institute, scientists found a single-celled creature from a pond at Oxford University Parks that breaks a near-universal rule of biology. All known life uses the same set of signals in its DNA to tell a cell when to stop reading a gene. This organism ignores them in a completely new way — the first such case ever found. It hints that the genetic code, once thought nearly universal across all life on Earth, may be far more flexible than biology has assumed. 📄 Published via Earlham Institute · More: https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260507024045.htm
